claims.claim: Moderate egg consumption (up to one egg per day) is not associated with incident cardiovascular disease risk in large US cohorts after adjusting for lifestyle and dietary factors.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Hazard ratio for at least one egg per day vs less than one egg per month
claim_quantities.source_quote: hazard ratio for at least one egg per day v less than one egg per month 0.93, 95% confidence interval 0.82 to 1.05

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Confidence interval for the hazard ratio of one egg per day
claim_quantities.source_quote: hazard ratio for at least one egg per day v less than one egg per month 0.93, 95% confidence interval 0.82 to 1.05

claims.importance_rationale: This is the primary finding of the three large prospective US cohort studies and addresses the core question of whether eggs are harmful to cardiovascular health.
claims.relevance_rationale: This is the primary finding of the three large prospective US cohort studies and addresses the core question of whether eggs are harmful to cardiovascular health.
claims.source_quote: In the pooled multivariable analysis, consumption of at least one egg per day was not associated with incident cardiovascular disease risk after adjustment for updated lifestyle and dietary factors associated with egg intake (hazard ratio for at least one egg per day v less than one egg per month 0.93, 95% confidence interval 0.82 to 1.05).

claim_context.evidence_design: Prospective cohort study (pooled analysis of three cohorts)
claim_context.exposure_or_option: at least one egg per day
claim_context.population: US health professionals (NHS, NHS II, HPFS)
claim_context.stated_dose_or_threshold: one egg per day vs less than one egg per month

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Hazard ratio for at least one egg per day vs less than one egg per month
claim_quantities.source_quote: hazard ratio for at least one egg per day v less than one egg per month 0.93, 95% confidence interval 0.82 to 1.05

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Confidence interval for the hazard ratio of one egg per day
claim_quantities.source_quote: hazard ratio for at least one egg per day v less than one egg per month 0.93, 95% confidence interval 0.82 to 1.05

must_preserve_terms: adjusted for lifestyle and dietary factors
whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Eating up to one egg per day does not appear to increase cardiovascular disease risk in US populations.
scope_conditions: adjusted for lifestyle and dietary factors
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: Moderate egg consumption (up to one egg per day) is not associated with cardiovascular disease risk in the general population, though a potential association with higher risk may exist for individuals with type 2 diabetes.

supporting_quotes.quote: In the pooled multivariable analysis, consumption of at least one egg per day was not associated with incident cardiovascular disease risk after adjustment for updated lifestyle and dietary factors associated with egg intake (hazard ratio for at least one egg per day v less than one egg per month 0.93, 95% confidence interval 0.82 to 1.05).

claims.claim: In Asian cohorts, an increase of one egg per day was associated with a lower risk of cardiovascular disease.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Relative risk for one egg per day increase in Asian cohorts
Confidence interval for the relative risk in Asian cohorts

claims.importance_rationale: Identifies a significant geographical heterogeneity in the results, specifically an inverse association in Asian populations.
claims.relevance_rationale: Identifies a significant geographical heterogeneity in the results, specifically an inverse association in Asian populations.
claims.source_quote: an inverse association was seen in Asian cohorts (0.92, 0.85 to 0.99, I2=44.8%).

stated_limitations: Potential residual confounding related to consumption patterns and socioeconomic status

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Relative risk for one egg per day increase in Asian cohorts
Confidence interval for the relative risk in Asian cohorts

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: In some studies of Asian populations, higher egg consumption was associated with lower cardiovascular disease risk.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: Moderate egg consumption (up to one egg per day) is not associated with cardiovascular disease risk in the general population, though a potential association with higher risk may exist for individuals with type 2 diabetes.

supporting_quotes.quote: an inverse association was seen in Asian cohorts (0.92, 0.85 to 0.99, I2=44.8%).

claims.claim: Higher egg consumption may be associated with a higher risk of cardiovascular disease in people with type 2 diabetes.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Relative risk for each egg per day increase in people with type 2 diabetes
Relative risk for high vs low intake in people with type 2 diabetes

claims.importance_rationale: Identifies a specific high-risk population (those with type 2 diabetes) where the association might be positive.
claims.relevance_rationale: Identifies a specific high-risk population (those with type 2 diabetes) where the association might be positive.
claims.source_quote: When we restricted the meta-analysis of egg consumption and cardiovascular disease risk to people with type 2 diabetes only (table 2), the pooled relative risk for each egg per day increase was 1.25 (95% confidence interval 0.99 to 1.59) and the pooled relative risk for high versus low intake was 1.40 (1.00 to 1.97).

claim_context.evidence_design: Meta-analysis of studies including people with type 2 diabetes
stated_limitations: Considerable heterogeneity between studies was observed concomitantly

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Relative risk for each egg per day increase in people with type 2 diabetes
Relative risk for high vs low intake in people with type 2 diabetes

must_preserve_terms: pooled relative risk for each egg per day increase was 1.25 (0.99 to 1.59)
whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: People with type 2 diabetes may face a higher cardiovascular risk with high egg consumption due to potential issues with cholesterol homeostasis.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: Moderate egg consumption (up to one egg per day) is not associated with cardiovascular disease risk in the general population, though a potential association with higher risk may exist for individuals with type 2 diabetes.

supporting_quotes.quote: When we restricted the meta-analysis of egg consumption and cardiovascular disease risk to people with type 2 diabetes only (table 2), the pooled relative risk for each egg per day increase was 1.25 (95% confidence interval 0.99 to 1.59) and the pooled relative risk for high versus low intake was 1.40 (1.00 to 1.97).

claims.claim: Replacing one whole egg per day with a serving of processed red meat or full-fat milk is associated with a higher risk of cardiovascular disease.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Hazard ratio for replacing eggs with processed red meat
claim_quantities.source_quote: processed red meat (hazard ratio 1.15, 95% confidence interval 1.05 to 1.27)

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Hazard ratio for replacing eggs with full fat milk

claims.importance_rationale: Provides a comparative context for egg consumption relative to other common dietary items.
claims.relevance_rationale: Provides a comparative context for egg consumption relative to other common dietary items.
claims.source_quote: We found a higher risk of cardiovascular disease when eggs were replaced with processed red meat (hazard ratio 1.15, 95% confidence interval 1.05 to 1.27), unprocessed red meat (1.10, 1.02 to 1.18), or full fat milk (1.11, 1.03 to 1.20).

claim_context.exposure_or_option: Replacing one whole egg per day with processed red meat or full fat milk
stated_limitations: Results from replacement analysis should be interpreted with caution in the context of statistical modeling.

claim_quantities.source_quote: processed red meat (hazard ratio 1.15, 95% confidence interval 1.05 to 1.27)

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Hazard ratio for replacing eggs with full fat milk

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Replacing eggs with processed red meat or full-fat milk may increase cardiovascular disease risk compared to eating eggs.
